Fnrn Polrrrcnl Pnacrrcrs CoMl,rrssroN
428 J Street r Suire 620 o Sacramento, CA g5gl4_232g
(916) 322-5660 o Fax (916) 322_0886
April 19,2010
Dave Kelley
l2T4Yalley Forge Drive
Chico, CA95973
Re
Your Request for Advice
Our file No. 10-060
Dear Mr. Kelley:
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the honorarium provisions of the
Political Reform Act (the "Act")'l Please note that our advice Is based ror"ry on the provisions
of the Act' We offer no opinion on the application of any other bodies of law or any
incompatible activities restrictions your agency may imptse.
QUESTTON
Are payments you would receive for writing non-fiction articles in a local newspaper
about l) a local architect, 2) local zoning and building code impacts on commercial building, and
3) design styles honoraria that rhe Act prohibits?
CONCLUSION
No' As discussed in more detail below, compensation received in exchange for articles
I:::f 
as part of your practice of a bona fide profession is earned income, not prohibited
nonorarla-
FACTS
You are a Planning Commissioner for the City of Chico, serving in your sixth year. you
are also a licensed architect with the firm Nichols, Mllburg, and Rosselto Architects in Chico,
cA' You occasionally researc! and write articles about orihit..tur", some of which are
published in local newspapers.2
' The Political Refbrm Act is contained in Government code sections g1000 through gl0l4. All statutory
ret'erences are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair political practices
commission are contained in Sections l8l l0 through I8997 of rirle 2 of thi California code of Regularions. All
regulatory ref'erences are to Title 2. Division 6 of thi califbrnia Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.


File No. A-10-060
Page No. 2
ATIALYSIS
The Act prohibits an elected or{11of a local govemmental agency from accepting an
honorarium from any source. (Section 89502(a).) Inc'iuded in the deinitiln of .,honorarium,, is,
"any payment made for any speech given, u.tifi. puuiirrr"d, or attendance at any public or
private conference, convention, .n."iing, social ";.;i;;".r, or like gathering.,, (Section
89501(a)') "Honorarium" does not include, howerer:'"Earned income for personal services
which are customarily provided in connection with the practice of a bona fide business, trade, or
profession, such as teaching, practicing law, medicine, insurance, real estate, banking, or
building contracting, unless the sole or predominant activity of the business, trade, or profession
is making speeches." (Section g9501(b).)
Regulation lg93l.2 also provides the following:
"(a) For purposes of Govemment code sections gg501 through gg506, an
'article published' means a nonfictional written work:
(l) That is produced in connection with any activity other than the practice
of a bona fide business, trade, or profession; and
(2) That is published in a periodicar, journar, newspaper, newsletter,
magazine, pamphlet, or s imilar publ itation.,;
You will be writing non-fiction articles for a Iocal newspaper. while this definition of
the "article published" for pulposes of the honorarium ban at section gg502is broad enough to
include periodic columns in a local newspaper, this fact is not dispositive of your question.
Periodic columns in a local newspaper mayfall under the exception in section gg50l(b)(l) if:
"(1) Theservices are provided in connection with an individual,s business
or the individual's practice of or employment in a bona fide business,
trade, or profession, such as-teaching, piacticing law, medicir", inrurun"",
rear estate, banking, or brrilding conliactirg, pursuant to [Regurations
18932.1 through 1 8932.31; and
(2) The services are customarily provided in connection with the business,
trade, or profession." (Regulatio; 1g932(a).)
A trade or professional practitioner is presumed to be "bona fide,, if ,.the tradesperson or
professional practitionet pott"si.s current Iicensure or certification which is issued by state or
federal occupational licensing authority, the issuance of which requires a demonstration of skill
and knowledge in connection with the relevant t.oa" - proi.ssion.,, (Regulation lgg32.l(c)(l).)
Based on the facts provided, you are a licensed and practicing architect. As a licensed
architect, you are presumed to be a "Lona fide" profesio*f proctitioner. The remaining
' Your city attorney wrote on your behalf in 2006 with a similar set of facrs (see Barker Advice Letter,
A- 06-r 88). With the further informat ron you provided, including article
are able to offer more complete advlce.
topics and your licensure information, we


File No. 4-10-060
Page No.3
question is whether periodic columns in a Iocal newspaper are "customarily provided,, in
connection with the business, trade or profession.
Generally, the determination ofwhether a particular speech or article is ..customarily
provided" in connection with a bona fid-e_trade or profession ii made on a case-by-case basis.
(See, e'g', Halderman Advice Letter, I-08-073 and references therein.) we have found that a
speech given or article published that relates to the public official's ur"u oi"*p"rtise is eamed
income not subject to the honorarium ban. (Id.,see also (JelmanAdvice Letter, A-00-2g1.) you
intend to write articles on architecture, architects and their styles, and other topics within your
expertise' Based on your position as a licensed architect and the direct correlation between that
and your articles, we view the articles as being customarily provided in your bona fide
profession' As such, the payments you receivi for these urti"l", wodJ;;;e protriuitea
honoraria, but would need to be disclosed on your statement of economic interests as earned
income if the payments exceed $500 0ver a li-month period. (Regulation 1g703.5.)
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.
Sincerely,
Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel
By: Heather M. Rowan
Counsel, Legal Division
Wl^^MW
HMRjgl
